title: legislation-7801489-attachment-Ex 1 - The Flood Insurance Study for King County Washington
gemma_headline: Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) Flood Insurance Study for King County, Washington
gemma_body:
This document serves as Volume 1 of the Flood Insurance Study (FIS) for King County, Washington, and its incorporated areas. Published by FEMA and last revised on August 19, 2020 (with corrections issued December 11, 2020), the study provides critical flood hazard data used for floodplain management and the administration of the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP).

The text includes a comprehensive list of participating communities and their corresponding identification numbers, ranging from major cities like Seattle and Bellevue to smaller towns and tribal lands. It advises users that this report may not contain all available data and encourages them to consult the Community Map Repository for the most current information, as FEMA may revise the study via the Letter of Map Revision process.

Key technical updates mentioned include:
*   **Zone Reclassifications:** Former flood hazard zone designations have been updated (e.g., Zones A1–A30 are now Zone AE; Zones B and C are now Zone X).
*   **Levee Warnings:** A specific alert is issued regarding several FIRM panels associated with the Green River. FEMA notes that certain levees have not yet been demonstrated to meet NFIP regulations (44 CFR 65.10) regarding their capacity to provide 1-percent-annual-chance flood protection, marking these as areas subject to potential data changes.

title: legislation-7801489-attachment-Ex 2 - Floodplain and Flood-prone Area Maps (1 of 8)
gemma_headline: FEMA Flood Insurance Rate Map (FIRM) Index Guidelines and Technical Specifications
gemma_body:
This document serves as a FIRM Index for floodplain and flood-prone area maps, specifically referencing data dated August 19, 2020. It provides essential instructions for users to ensure they are utilizing the most current mapping data for land use and insurance purposes.

Key points include:
*   **Verification of Current Maps:** Because the Index may not be redistributed following subsequent revisions, users are directed to verify the current map dates for specific FIRM panels via the FEMA Map Service Center website or the FEMA Map Information exchange (FMIX) phone line.
*   **Annexation Requirements:** Communities annexing land on adjacent FIRM panels are required to obtain the most current copies of both the adjacent panels and the FIRM Index.
*   **Distribution Policy:** Future revisions to the Index will be issued only to communities located on panels that are actually being revised. Consequently, this Index remains valid for panels dated August 19, 2020, or earlier.
*   **Technical Specifications:** 
    *   **Base Map Source:** The map data was derived from multiple sources, including digital files provided by King County, with a scale of 1:24,000 produced between 1994 and 2012.
    *   **Elevation Datum:** Flood elevations are referenced to the North American Vertical Datum of 1988 (NAVD 88). Users are directed to the National Geodetic Survey (NGS) for further information regarding vertical datum conversions.

title: legislation-7801489-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
gemma_headline: City of Seattle to Adopt Permanent FEMA Floodplain Regulations and Updated Rate Maps
gemma_body:
This legislation proposes the adoption of permanent Floodplain Development regulations and the formal integration of the February 2020 National Flood Insurance Rate Maps (NFIRMs) into the Seattle Municipal Code. These updates are required to maintain compliance with Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) regulations (44 CFR 60.3).

The primary purpose of these updates is to ensure that property owners in flood-prone areas can continue to obtain federally backed mortgages and purchase or renew flood insurance through FEMA’s Flood Insurance Program. While the City has operated under interim regulations and maps since August 2020 (which have been extended nine times), this ordinance makes those standards permanent. The permanent maps are identical to the interim maps previously used.

Key details include:
*   **Scope:** The regulations apply to all permit applications for construction within FEMA-mapped floodplain areas. This includes projects managed by various city departments, such as Seattle Public Utilities (SPU), Seattle Department of Transportation (SDOT), and Parks.
*   **Land Impact:** The updated mapping represents a net increase of 873 acres (89 acres of land and 784 acres of water).
*   **Fiscal Impact:** There is no direct financial cost to the City. The Seattle Department of Construction and Inspections (SDCI) has determined that existing staff can handle the permit reviews and that costs will be recovered through existing permit fees.
*   **Risk of Inaction:** Failure to adopt these permanent regulations could prevent property owners in mapped floodplains from accessing essential insurance and mortgage services.
*   **Procedural Requirements:** The legislation requires a public hearing and the publication of a notice in the Daily Journal of Commerce and/or The Seattle Times.

title: legislation-7801489-supporting-Director's Report
gemma_headline: Seattle Proposed Amendments to Permanent Floodplain Development Regulations
gemma_body:
This Director’s Report, dated December 2, 2025, outlines necessary updates to Seattle’s floodplain development regulations to ensure compliance with federal and state mandates. While the city has long maintained regulations requiring new buildings in special flood hazard areas to be engineered for flood resistance and elevated above anticipated flood levels, a 2020 audit by the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) identified several gaps in the current city code.

To meet the minimum standards of the Code of Federal Regulations (CFR), RCW 86.16, and WAC 173-158, the city is proposing the following amendments:
*   **Definitions:** Updating and adding new definitions to align with regulatory standards.
*   **Development Standards:** Updating regulations regarding development within regulatory floodways.
*   **Coastal High Hazard Zones:** Incorporating regulations for the newly mapped "VE Zone" (coastal high hazard flood zones).

The report notes that the 2020 FEMA Flood Insurance Rate Maps (FIRM) have expanded the regulated floodplain area by 873 acres. These updates affect various regions, including the Duwamish River and Waterway, the Central Waterfront piers, Smith Cove, and areas surrounding Thornton and Longfellow creeks. 

Property owners can determine if their land is subject to these regulations by consulting the FEMA FIRM maps, the City’s specific flood-prone maps, King County’s iMap, or the SDCI GIS database.

title: legislation-7801489-supporting-Director's Report App A - Instructions for using IMap
baseline_headline: Instructions for using King County’s IMap for FEMA floodplain mapping information
baseline_body: Directors Report App A outlines how to find property addresses in FEMA floodplain areas via King County’s IMap.
gemma_headline: Instructions for Determining FEMA Flood Zone Designations via King County iMap
gemma_body:
This document provides a step-by-step guide for users to determine if a specific property is located within a FEMA-designated Special Flood Hazard Area (SFHA) and to identify its specific Flood Zone Designation using the King County iMap tool. 

The process involves three primary steps:
1. Accessing the King County iMap website.
2. Searching for the specific property address using the search tool.
3. Enabling the “Flood Info” layer via the layer list menu.

Once the layer is active, users can click on the property parcel to confirm if it is within an SFHA and use the right arrow icon to view the detailed flood zone classification. If the system returns "no information" or if the user is unable to determine if the property falls within specific zones (such as A, AE, AO, or VE), they are instructed to contact Margaret Glowacki at the Seattle Department of Construction and Inspections (SDCI) via email or phone.

gemma_headline: City of Seattle 2020 Updated FEMA Floodplain Mapping Report
gemma_body:
This document is "Appendix B" of a Director's Report, providing a series of technical maps (Figures 3 through 5) that identify all city parcels subject to floodplain regulations based on updated 2020 FEMA data. The maps serve to visualize flood-prone areas and the specific FEMA designations (such as Zones A, AE, AH, AO, VE, and X) applied to various land parcels.

The report highlights two primary geographic "Focus Areas": the Duwamish area and the Thornton Creek area. A significant portion of the documentation is dedicated to illustrating "designation changes," specifically comparing 1995 data to 2020 data. These maps pinpoint specific regions where land was reclassified from Zone X (typically lower risk) to higher-risk categories, such as Zone As* or Zone VE. The data was produced in 2024 by EA Engineering, Science, and Technology, Inc., PBC, for the City of Seattle.

title: legislation-7801489-supporting-Director's Report App C - Definitions of FEMA Flood Zone Designations
baseline_body: FEMA Flood Zone Designations are geographic areas with varying flood risk levels depicted on FIRM/FHR maps. High Risk areas include Coastal Areas with storm wave hazards, Moderate to Low Risk Zones include base floodplains and shallow flooding areas, and Undetermined Risk Areas have specific legal requirements for flood control systems.
gemma_headline: Overview of FEMA Flood Zone Designations and Insurance Requirements
gemma_body:
This document provides a comprehensive guide to the flood zone designations established by FEMA, which are used on Flood Insurance Rate Maps (FIRM) and Flood Hazard Boundary Maps to identify the severity and type of flood risk in a specific geographic area. The designations are categorized by risk level, which determines whether flood insurance is optional or mandatory for participants in the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP).

**Moderate to Low Risk Areas**
In these zones, flood insurance is available but not mandatory:
*   **Zones B and X (shaded):** Areas of moderate hazard, typically located between the 100-year and 500-year flood limits. This includes areas protected by levees or shallow flooding areas.
*   **Zones C and X (unshaded):** Areas of minimal hazard, generally above the 500-year flood level.

**High Risk Areas**
These zones carry a mandatory flood insurance purchase requirement. Most of these areas have a 1% annual chance of flooding (a 26% chance over a 30-year mortgage):
*   **Zone A:** High risk, but lacks detailed depth or base flood elevation (BFE) data.
*   **Zones AE and A1-30:** The base floodplain where specific BFEs are provided (AE is the current format; A1-30 is the older format).
*   **Zones AH and AO:** Areas prone to shallow flooding (1 to 3 feet), with AH referring to ponding and AO referring to river/stream sheet flow.
*   **Zone AR:** Areas with temporary risk increases due to the construction or restoration of flood control systems.
*   **Zone A99:** Areas with a 1% annual risk that will be protected by a Federal flood control system.

**High Risk Coastal Areas**
These zones also require mandatory insurance and are characterized by a 1% or greater annual chance of flooding combined with the additional hazard of storm waves:
*   **Zone V:** High-risk coastal areas without provided base flood elevations.
*   **Zones VE and V1-30:** High-risk coastal areas where base flood elevations are provided.

**Undetermined Risk Areas**
*   **Zone D:** Areas where the risk level has not yet been determined.

title: legislation-7801489-supporting-Director's Report App D - FEMA Code Review
baseline_headline: Seattle's Floodplain Development Ordinance Expands FEMA Code Review Mandate
baseline_body: The Seattle Floodplain Development Ordinance expands FEMA Code Review responsibilities to promote public health, safety, and general welfare, mandate more flood protection measures, and communicate flood risks to property buyers and residents.
gemma_headline: FEMA Review of Seattle’s Floodplain Development Regulations
gemma_body:
This document is an excerpt from a Director's Report (Appendix D) detailing a FEMA code review of Seattle’s Floodplain Development Regulations. The review compares Seattle's existing municipal code against the Washington State Model Ordinance and the Code of Federal Regulations to ensure compliance and safety.

The provided section focuses on "Section 1.0 - Statutory Authorization, Findings of Fact, Purpose, and Objectives." While FEMA notes that adopting this specific section is not mandatory, they strongly recommend that Seattle incorporate the following elements into its Floodplain Development Ordinance:

*   **Statutory Authorization:** Establishing the legal delegation of responsibility from the State of Washington to the local community to manage floodplains for public health and safety.
*   **Findings of Fact:** Formally acknowledging that flood hazard areas are subject to periodic inundation, which can lead to loss of life, property damage, and economic disruption.
*   **Statement of Purpose:** Defining the goals of the ordinance, which include protecting human life, reducing the cost of flood insurance, minimizing public expenditure on rescue and relief, and protecting critical infrastructure (utilities, streets, and bridges).
*   **Methods of Loss Reduction:** Implementing strategies to restrict dangerous development, requiring flood protection for vulnerable constructions, and controlling the alteration of natural floodplains and stream channels.

The document utilizes a color-coding system to indicate status: red text signifies areas requiring attention, while green text indicates compliance with the Seattle code (specifically referencing sections 25.06.015, 25.06.020, and 25.06.022).

title: legislation-7801489-full
baseline_body: The Seattle City Council passed a comprehensive ordinance to update floodplain management regulations, aligning with Federal FEMA standards and renewing interim floodplain regulations for continued NFIP participation.
gemma_headline: City of Seattle Moves to Establish Permanent Floodplain Management Regulations and Adopt Updated FEMA Maps
gemma_body:
This legislative text is a proposed ordinance intended to transition the City of Seattle from temporary, interim floodplain regulations to permanent city code. The primary objective is to ensure the City remains in compliance with the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) and the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP), as well as Washington State Department of Ecology standards.

Remaining a participating community in the NFIP is critical because it allows local property owners, renters, and businesses—particularly those with government-backed mortgages in high-risk areas—to obtain necessary flood insurance. To maintain this eligibility, the City must adopt and enforce updated National Flood Insurance Rate Maps (FIRM) and a new Flood Insurance Study (FIS).

The text notes that since July 2020, the City has relied on a series of short-term interim ordinances (including Ordinances 126113 and 126536) to regulate development in special flood hazard areas. These interim measures were repeatedly extended over several years to avoid a gap in compliance. This new ordinance seeks to repeal those temporary measures and formally amend Chapter 25.06 and Section 25.09.030 of the Seattle Municipal Code to make these flood management regulations permanent.

title: legislation-7858034-attachment-Appointment Packet
baseline_headline: Confirmation of Aly Pennucci as City Budget Director
baseline_body: Aly Pennucci is appointed as City Budget Director, taking office on January 9, 2026.
gemma_headline: Mayor Katie B. Wilson Nominates Aly Pennucci for City Budget Director
gemma_body:
On January 9, 2026, Mayor Katie B. Wilson submitted a formal confirmation packet to Seattle City Council President Joy Hollingsworth requesting the appointment of Aly Pennucci as the City Budget Director and Director of the City Budget Office (CBO).

Ms. Pennucci is a veteran public policy and fiscal management leader with over 17 years of experience. Her professional background includes serving as the Deputy Executive for Whatcom County—where she improved budget transparency and identified new revenue sources—and nearly a decade as the Deputy Director for the Seattle City Council's Central Staff, where she managed the review and adoption of the city's multi-billion-dollar budget.

The appointment carries an annual salary of $242,354. If confirmed by the City Council, Ms. Pennucci will serve an initial four-year term ending on December 31, 2029. The confirmation process includes a review of her professional credentials and background check, as well as a review by Councilmember Strauss’ Finance, Native Communities & Tribal Governments Committee.

title: legislation-7858034-attachment-Written Questions and Responses
baseline_body: I left Seattle for a chance to lead an Executive team in Whatcom County, appreciating public governance under fiscal stress and budget clarity. My return is for Seattle’s challenges, inspired by budget collaboration and transparency, aiming to improve city’s fiscal health and community services.
gemma_headline: Appointment Questionnaire for Interim Director of the City Budget Office, Aly Pennucci
gemma_body:
In this legislative text, Interim Director Aly Pennucci outlines her motivations for returning to the City of Seattle and her philosophy regarding municipal budgeting. Having previously served in Seattle and more recently in the Whatcom County Executive team, Pennucci emphasizes that her experience managing public enterprises under fiscal restraint has solidified her passion for local government finance and the necessity of making budget processes accessible to the public.

Pennucci identifies a critical tension in Seattle: the city is wealthy, yet it faces a structural budget deficit and an expanding income gap. She expresses a commitment to Mayor Wilson’s vision of increasing affordability, compassion for vulnerable populations, and overall transparency.

Regarding the budget process, Pennucci argues that success depends on collaboration between the Legislative and Executive branches. She cites the coordinated distribution of federal COVID-19 response funding and the use of user-friendly budget tracking websites as examples of effective practice. Conversely, she asserts that the process fails when information is incomplete or when policy decisions are presented as unavoidable realities. She concludes by advocating for a transparent approach that clearly articulates the reasoning behind financial decisions to maintain public and inter-branch trust.

title: legislation-7808079-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
baseline_headline: SPU Bitter Lake Reservoir PTOJ SUM: Transfer of Right-of-Way to SDOT
baseline_body:
The Seattle Public Utilities (SPU) is transferring partial jurisdiction of a Bitter Lake Reservoir property to the Seattle Department of Transportation (SDOT), for sidewalk improvements on Linden Avenue North. This legislative action aligns with SPU's commitment to modernize utility operations while opening part of the site for public use.

FISCAL NOTE: The legislation does not affect the City's budget directly; instead, it authorizes the transfer of jurisdiction to SDOT for specific sidewalk-related improvements. 

OTHER IMPLICATIONS:
a. No public hearing is required.
b. No publication of notice with The Daily Journal of Commerce or The Seattle Times is needed.
c. The legislation does not affect a property outside of SPU's Bitter Lake Reservoir; SDOT will handle the sidewalk improvements on Linden Avenue North. 

[Attachment: Exhibit A - Bitter Lake Reservoir Replacement Project Details]
gemma_headline: Transfer of Jurisdiction for Bitter Lake Reservoir Property to Expand Public Sidewalks
gemma_body:
This legislation proposes an ordinance to transfer partial jurisdiction of a small portion of the Bitter Lake Reservoir property from Seattle Public Utilities (SPU) to the Seattle Department of Transportation (SDOT). 

The transfer is part of the larger Bitter Lake Reservoir Replacement Project, which involves replacing an aging floating cover with a new, partially buried reinforced concrete reservoir. As part of this project, SPU is required to upgrade the sidewalk on Linden Avenue North (between North 138th and North 143rd Streets) to meet current standards by widening it to six feet. 

To achieve this, a 610-square-foot area of SPU-owned land is required to be designated as right-of-way for street and sidewalk purposes. SPU has determined that this specific sliver of land is not necessary for current or future utility operations. The legislation indicates that there are no financial impacts to the City of Seattle and no public hearing or formal publication of notice is required.

title: legislation-7808079-supporting-Summary Ex B - Racial Equity Toolkit
baseline_headline: SPU Racial Equity Toolkit for Bitter Lake Reservoir Covering Improvements
baseline_body: The Racial Equity Toolkit for Bitter Lake Reservoir Covering Improvements, developed by SPU, involves comprehensive engagement to address equity and prevent disparities in service, program, or policy development.
gemma_headline: Inclusive Engagement Plan for Bitter Lake Reservoir Covering Improvements
gemma_body:
This document is a Racial Equity Toolkit (Inclusive Engagement Plan) developed by Seattle Public Utilities (SPU) for the Bitter Lake Reservoir Covering Improvements project. Managed by Andrew Karch and a multidisciplinary team, the plan outlines the strategy to ensure the project is implemented with a focus on racial equity and community inclusivity.

Key details of the plan include:

*   **Geographic Focus:** The project's impacts are neighborhood-specific, primarily affecting the Bitter Lake neighborhood in the Northwest region of the city.
*   **Expertise and Collaboration:** The project team is collaborating with SPU Community Affairs and Environmental Justice and Service Equity (EJSE) experts, as well as staff from the North Shop, the Office of Arts & Culture, Parks and Recreation, and the Department of Neighborhoods.
*   **Stakeholder Engagement:** The team has identified that all stakeholders require tailored outreach, with a specific emphasis on providing translation services for non-English speakers. 
*   **Community Sentiment:** Initial feedback from community leaders and stakeholder interviews indicates general support for burying the reservoir. However, the team has noted specific community concerns regarding accessibility, the preservation of the local p-patch, and safety for residents along Freemont Ave (specifically the maintenance of the west-side fence).
*   **Current Status and Next Steps:** The project has received unconditional approval from the Seattle Design Commission. The team is currently organizing its first open-house outreach event and intends to utilize translation services, interpretation, and community liaisons to ensure the engagement process is culturally relevant and linguistically accessible to all residents.

title: legislation-7808079-supporting-Presentation
baseline_headline: Transfer of partial jurisdiction from SPU to SDOT for Bitter Lake Reservoir Replacement Project
baseline_body: The Seattle Public Utilities transferred approximately 610 square feet of jurisdiction to Seattle Department of Transportation for the Bitter Lake Reservoir Replacement Project to support sidewalk improvements along Linden Ave N.
gemma_headline: Seattle City Council Ordinance for Partial Transfer of Jurisdiction: Bitter Lake Reservoir Replacement Project
gemma_body:
This legislative proposal seeks authorization to transfer a small portion of land jurisdiction from Seattle Public Utilities (SPU) to the Seattle Department of Transportation (SDOT). The transfer involves a 1-foot-wide strip of land (approximately 610 square feet) adjacent to the existing right-of-way along Linden Ave N. This transfer is necessary to meet SDOT sidewalk standards, which require a 6-foot width for pedestrian improvements. SPU has determined that this specific area is no longer required for future utility operations.

This jurisdictional change is part of the broader Bitter Lake Reservoir Replacement Project, which includes the construction of a new concrete reservoir and various community enhancements. These improvements include street upgrades (paved parking and sidewalks on N 143rd St and Linden Ave N, and a trail on N 138th St), the creation of a new park via Seattle Parks and Recreation, the installation of public art, and the expansion of a P-patch. The project has been developed through extensive community engagement, utilizing equity toolkits, design outreach, and crime prevention (CPTED) consultations.

title: legislation-7808079-full
baseline_headline: City of Seattle Authorizes SDOT to Transfer BLR Reservoir Property for Transportation Purposes
baseline_body: The City Council authorizes SDOT to take over a 610 square foot portion of SPU's BLR property for transportation improvements along Linden Avenue North, aligning utility and pedestrian infrastructure needs while transferring jurisdiction to SDOT.
gemma_headline: City of Seattle Ordinance for Jurisdictional Transfer of Bitter Lake Reservoir Property to SDOT
gemma_body:
This ordinance authorizes the transfer of a small portion of land (approximately 610 square feet) from Seattle Public Utilities (SPU) to the Seattle Department of Transportation (SDOT) as part of the Bitter Lake Reservoir Replacement Project. 

The project stems from SPU's decision in 2022 to replace the aging floating cover of the Bitter Lake Reservoir with a new, partially buried reinforced concrete structure, which will allow more of the property to be opened for public use. To comply with SDOT street improvement standards, the sidewalk along Linden Avenue North (between North 143rd Street and North 138th Street) must be widened to 6 feet. 

Because SPU has determined that this specific "Transfer Area" is no longer needed for utility purposes, the ordinance designates the land for street and sidewalk use and officially establishes it as public right-of-way. The transfer is a jurisdictional shift between city departments; therefore, no monetary compensation is required. The ordinance further mandates that SPU and SDOT coordinate to ensure these improvements align with long-term community benefit goals and authorizes the respective department heads to execute the necessary documentation to finalize the transfer.

title: legislation-7872965-attachment-Att 1 - Water Supply Forum Interlocal Agreement 2026-2035
baseline_headline: An Interlocal Agreement for the Puget Sound Water Forum
baseline_body: The Puget Sound Water Forum, comprising Seattle, Everett, Tacoma, and Cascade, has established an Interlocal Agreement (ILA) to manage its collaborative efforts on water supply issues over a 5-year period, ensuring effective governance and inclusivity among its member utilities and organizations.
gemma_headline: Interlocal Agreement for the Water Supply Forum (2026-2035)
gemma_body:
This legislative text establishes a formal Interlocal Agreement (ILA) for the Water Supply Forum, a collaborative association of drinking water suppliers and purveyors serving King, Snohomish, and Pierce counties in the Puget Sound region. Replacing a previous Memorandum of Agreement that expires at the end of 2025, this new agreement utilizes the RCW 39.34 Interlocal Cooperation Act to provide a legal framework for funding and coordinating regional water resource projects.

The primary goal of the Forum is to collaborate on mutual interests, including water supply planning, seismic resiliency, water quality, conservation, and public outreach. 

The agreement defines three tiers of participation:
*   **Regional Members:** Consisting of the City of Seattle, the City of Everett, the City of Tacoma, and the Cascade Water Alliance. These members hold primary governance roles, including the ability to serve as Chair or Vice-Chair and sit on the Steering Committee.
*   **Fee Members:** Open to counties or utilities/associations with 10,000 or more connections. These members pay an annual fee of $1,000 and maintain voting rights and the ability to lead topic-focused committees.
*   **Guests:** Open to other agencies and individuals. Guests do not pay dues or vote, though they may participate in specific committees if invited.

title: legislation-7872965-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
baseline_headline: Seattle Public Utilities ILA SUM: Interlocal Agreement Update
baseline_body: The updated interlocal agreement empowers the Seattle Public Utilities to collaborate with regional water suppliers and associates in the Puget Sound region.
gemma_headline: Ordinance Authorizing Seattle Public Utilities to Enter Interlocal Agreement for Regional Water Supply Forum
gemma_body:
This legislation authorizes the General Manager and CEO of Seattle Public Utilities (SPU) to enter into an interlocal agreement (ILA) to participate in the regional Water Supply Forum. Established in 1999, the Forum is a collaborative association of drinking water suppliers and leaders from King, Snohomish, and Pierce counties focused on regional water supply planning, system resiliency, water quality, operations, and conservation.

While the Forum previously operated under informal funding agreements and Memorandums of Agreement, members have determined that an ILA is the most appropriate legal vehicle for managing funding and administration moving forward. 

Key components of the agreement include:
*   **Membership and Governance:** The Steering Committee consists of four primary regional members: the City of Seattle, the City of Tacoma, the City of Everett, and the Cascade Water Alliance. Other counties, associations, and utilities may participate as fee-based members.
*   **Administration:** The City of Tacoma will continue to serve as the Forum’s administrator, handling invoicing, fund management, vendor contracts, and fiscal reporting under the direction of the Steering Committee.
*   **Funding Structure:** Dues are determined by an annual work plan and budget. When dues are required, costs are allocated based on service area and connection volume: Seattle (35%), Tacoma (25%), Everett (25%), and Cascade (15%). 
*   **Future Governance:** The Forum intends to adopt formal bylaws in 2026 to establish detailed operating practices.

**Financial Impact:** There is no immediate financial impact for 2026, as the Forum has a healthy balance and has not collected dues in over five years. If dues are required in the future, the City of Seattle will pay approximately 35% of the costs from the Water Fund. Based on mid-2010s data, this is estimated at roughly $50,000 per year, which would be absorbed into the existing budget authority.

title: legislation-7872965-supporting-Presentation
baseline_headline: Seattle Interlocal Agreement for Water Supply Forum
baseline_body: The Seattle Interlocal Agreement for the Regional Water Supply Forum was enacted on February 12, 2026, empowering Seattle Public Utilities to negotiate with City of Everett, City of Tacoma, and Cascade Water Alliance for a 10-year water supply partnership. This collaboration aims to advance water supply planning, coordination, and resource sharing among the three participating cities and their water associations.
gemma_headline: Seattle Public Utilities Seeks Authorization for Regional Water Supply Forum Interlocal Agreement (2026-2035)
gemma_body:
Seattle Public Utilities (SPU) is requesting authorization for its General Manager to enter into a formal interlocal agreement (ILA) with the City of Everett, the City of Tacoma, and the Cascade Water Alliance. This agreement will formalize the governance of the Regional Water Supply Forum for a ten-year period spanning 2026 to 2035.

The Water Supply Forum has operated as an informal association since 1999, serving as a collaborative hub for regional drinking water suppliers to coordinate on critical issues, including water supply planning, system resiliency, emergency preparedness, water quality, operations, and conservation. 

The Forum utilizes a three-tiered membership structure:
*   **Regional Members:** Seattle, Tacoma, Everett, and Cascade.
*   **Fee Members:** Smaller entities such as King County and the water districts of Covington and Alderwood.
*   **Guests:** Regulatory bodies, such as the Department of Health.

The proposed ILA replaces previous Memorandums of Understanding (MOUs) that expired in 2025. Under the new agreement, the City of Tacoma will continue to serve as the fiscal agent. Seattle will participate in the Steering Committee, rotate into the chair position, and contribute approximately 35% of the collected dues. The Forum is expected to adopt formal operational bylaws in 2026.

title: legislation-7872965-full
baseline_headline: Approves interlocal agreement for water supply collaboration.
baseline_body: The City of Seattle authorizes an interlocal agreement with local governments to collaborate on regional drinking water issues.
gemma_headline: City of Seattle Authorizes Interlocal Agreement for Regional Water Supply Forum
gemma_body:
This ordinance authorizes the General Manager and CEO of Seattle Public Utilities to enter into a formal interlocal agreement with the City of Everett, the City of Tacoma, and the Cascade Water Alliance. The purpose of this agreement is to formalize the City of Seattle's participation in the Water Supply Forum, a collaborative body that has operated informally since 1999.

The Forum is designed to coordinate and collaborate on drinking water supply issues and resource projects that benefit residents across King, Snohomish, and Pierce counties. While the group previously operated under informal funding agreements and a Memorandum of Agreement (expiring December 31, 2025), the members have determined that an interlocal agreement under chapter 39.34 RCW is the most appropriate legal vehicle for managing their shared funding and consultant activities.

The ordinance specifically authorizes the execution of the "Water Supply Forum Interlocal Agreement 2026-2035," ratifies any consistent actions taken prior to the ordinance's effective date, and establishes the timeline for the agreement to take effect.

title: legislation-7872966-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
baseline_headline: Seattle Public Utilities Zoe Loutos Akshay Iyengar Approves Imazapyr for Knotweed Control
baseline_body: This legislation amends the Secondary Use Policies to allow limited imazapyr herbicide use to treat knotweed in the Cedar River Municipal Watershed, aligning with ecological risks and transitioning to biennial treatment schedules.
gemma_headline: Ordinance to Extend Limited Herbicide Use for Invasive Knotweed Control in Cedar River Municipal Watershed
gemma_body:
This legislation proposes an amendment to the Secondary Use Policies of the Cedar River Municipal Watershed (originally established by Ordinance 114632 in 1989) to allow for the continued, limited application of the herbicide imazapyr. 

The primary objective is to combat the spread of invasive knotweed, a noxious weed that threatens water quality and destroys fish and wildlife habitats by eroding stream banks and displacing native plants. Seattle Public Utilities (SPU) determined that manual removal methods—such as digging and the use of geotextile fabrics—were too costly, labor-intensive, and risked further spreading the weed via root fragments.

While the 1989 ordinance established a general moratorium on herbicides to prevent broadcast spraying, the City Council has previously granted short-term exceptions (every three years) for the selective use of imazapyr. This new legislation seeks to extend that authorization for six years, through 2031. This longer timeframe reflects a shift in SPU's management strategy to a biennial control schedule, where half of the infested sites are treated each year.

Financially, the legislation is expected to result in long-term cost savings compared to manual removal. These savings will be redirected toward surveying high-risk areas and controlling other priority noxious weeds. The document notes that no effective control alternative exists if this authority is not granted.

title: legislation-7872966-supporting-Summary Att 1 - Map of 2025 Knotweed distribution in the Cedar River Municipal Watershed
baseline_body: The 2025 knotweed map illustrates City ownership in the Cedar River watershed, specifically from Cascades crest to Landsburg Dam.
gemma_headline: 2025 Knotweed Distribution Map for the Cedar River Municipal Watershed
gemma_body: This document serves as a summary attachment (Exhibit A) providing a map of the distribution of knotweed within the Cedar River Municipal Watershed for the year 2025. Additionally, it clarifies the boundaries of city land ownership, noting that the city's jurisdiction extends from the crest of the Cascades to the Landsburg Dam in the Maple Valley area.

title: legislation-7872966-supporting-Presentation
baseline_headline: Renew imazapyr authority for cedar river knotweed control
baseline_body: Seattle Public Utilities seeks to renew imazapyr use in cedar river watershed with a 6-year cycle to manage knotweed.
gemma_headline: Seattle Public Utilities Seeks Renewal of Authority for Knotweed Control in Cedar River Municipal Watershed
gemma_body:
Seattle Public Utilities (SPU) is requesting that the Seattle City Council renew its authority to use the herbicide imazapyr to manage invasive knotweed within the Cedar River Municipal Watershed. SPU is legally mandated to control knotweed, which threatens the ecosystem by crowding out native vegetation and increasing erosion.

The proposal is based on a history of trial and error; between 2002 and 2010, non-chemical methods such as digging and fabric covering proved unsuccessful. However, since the City Council began authorizing the use of imazapyr in 2010, the program has seen significant success, with knotweed coverage and herbicide use decreasing by 95% since 2011.

To ensure safety and environmental protection, SPU notes that imazapyr targets an enzyme found only in plants, conducts water quality sampling before and after application, and avoids spraying when pollinators are present. Current management involves annual surveys and biennial treatment of long-term sites.

The proposed legislation seeks to extend the ordinance cycle to six years. SPU anticipates a reduction in the intensity of treatment, projecting that the amount of herbicide used over the next six years will be roughly equivalent to what was previously used in only three years.

title: legislation-7872966-full
baseline_headline: City amends Cedar River Watershed Secondary Use Policy to allow imazapyr use.
baseline_body: The City of Seattle amends its Cedar River Watershed Secondary Use Policy to allow the use of imazapyr for knotweed control, maintaining compliance with regulations and minimizing ecological risks.
gemma_headline: City of Seattle Extends Herbicide Use Exception to Combat Invasive Knotweed in Cedar River Watershed
gemma_body:
This ordinance amends the Secondary Use Policies of the Cedar River Municipal Watershed to extend a limited exception to the city's general herbicide moratorium. Specifically, it authorizes Seattle Public Utilities (SPU) to continue using the herbicide imazapyr to control and eradicate invasive knotweed species through December 31, 2031.

The legislation notes that while a moratorium on routine herbicide use was established in 1989, the rapid spread of knotweed—which threatens water quality, erodes stream banks, and displaces native flora—necessitated a more aggressive control strategy. Since 2010, a series of temporary exceptions have allowed for the use of imazapyr, which is cited as being relatively non-toxic to animals and posing negligible risk to water quality.

To ensure environmental safety and regulatory compliance, the ordinance mandates the following strict application protocols:
* **Targeted Application:** SPU must use a one percent imazapyr solution applied directly to leaves via handheld sprayers or backpacks (avoiding broadcast spraying).
* **Professional Oversight:** Only licensed pesticide applicators may apply the herbicide.
* **Monitoring:** SPU is required to conduct annual monitoring of treatment areas and perform water quality tests both before and after application.
* **Reporting:** SPU will provide updates to the Governance and Utilities Committee (or its successor).

The ordinance concludes that the ecological risk of limited imazapyr use is significantly lower than the risk posed by uncontrolled knotweed, and that this long-term strategy ensures the city remains in compliance with King County and state noxious weed laws.

title: legislation-7873173-supporting-Presentation
baseline_headline: Seattle Information Technology extends Comcast franchise
baseline_body: On February 12, 2026, Seattle Information Technology, representing the City of Seattle, extended Comcast's cable franchise for seven years, ensuring continued community benefits and addressing franchise renewal processes in a comprehensive review.
gemma_headline: Proposed Extension and Amendment of the City of Seattle and Comcast Cable Franchise Agreement
gemma_body:
The City of Seattle, through Seattle Information Technology, is proposing legislation to amend and extend its cable franchise agreement with Comcast. The proposed agreement would extend the franchise for seven years, moving the expiration date to January 20, 2033.

To inform the negotiations, the City conducted a comprehensive community assessment involving resident surveys, listening sessions, a technical review, and an audit of the Cable Customer Bill of Rights. This data revealed that while cable TV usage is declining overall (with only about one-third of households subscribing), it remains most prevalent among older adults (65+) and middle-to-lower-income residents.

The primary goal of the new legislation is to maintain and enhance community benefits while addressing the financial impact of "cord cutting." Key changes and priorities include:

*   **Increased Fees:** To support the "Internet for All" program and the Seattle Channel, the City is raising the Franchise Fee from 4.4% to 5.0% and the PEG (Public, Education, and Government) Fee from 0.4% to 0.6%. These increases are projected to generate additional revenues of approximately $606,878 in 2026 and $570,704 in 2027.
*   **Preservation of Benefits:** The agreement seeks to maintain existing benefits, including low-income discounts, complimentary service for schools and City buildings, and the "Access for All" program providing free internet to non-profits.
*   **Equity and Quality:** The City aims to promote affordability and equity while ensuring that PEG signal quality remains comparable to commercial channels.

The proposal follows a standard renewal process governed by Federal law and the Seattle Cable Code, ensuring that Comcast continues to meet technical, privacy, and customer service standards.

title: legislation-7921772-supporting-Presentation
gemma_headline: Seattle Public Library 2026 Budget and Levy Funding Overview
gemma_body:
This document provides a comprehensive financial and operational overview of the Seattle Public Library (SPL), specifically focusing on the role of the Library Levy in supporting its 2026 budget.

**Financial Overview**
For 2026, the SPL has an operating budget of $102.5 million and a capital budget of $2.6 million. The funding is derived from three primary sources: the General Fund, the Library Levy, and private funds (SPLF). Data from 2020 to 2026 shows a steady increase in the total budget, rising from $81.1 million to $110.1 million. While the General Fund remains the largest contributor, the percentage of funding provided by the General Fund has decreased over time (from 70.1% in 2020 to 60.6% in 2026), while the reliance on the Library Levy and private funding has grown.

**Levy Allocations**
The Library Levy is distributed across several critical categories to maintain library operations:
*   **Collections:** The largest expenditure area, covering books, digital materials, and specialized archives.
*   **Hours & Access:** Funding to ensure branches remain open and accessible to the public.
*   **Technology & Online Services:** Supporting cybersecurity, network infrastructure, and public internet access.
*   **Helping Children:** Dedicated funding for literacy and early learning.
*   **Building Maintenance:** Covering both routine facility support and major repairs.
*   **Administration:** Central costs and city-level administrative requirements.

**Collections and Accomplishments**
A significant portion of the presentation highlights the growth and diversification of the library's collections. Key achievements include:
*   **Digital Growth:** SPL is now one of the top 10 largest e-circulation libraries globally, with e-book checkouts nearly doubling since 2019.
*   **Inventory Expansion:** Total items have increased from 2.4 million to 2.9 million since 2019.
*   **Inclusivity & Access:** The library has expanded language access by adding collections in Dari, Pashto, Portuguese, and Ukrainian, and ensuring Spanish collections are available at all locations.
*   **Specialized Content:** The library maintains curated collections (e.g., LGBTQ+, African American,

title: legislation-7921772-supporting-Presentation v2 (added; 2/19/26)
gemma_headline: Seattle Public Library 2026 Budget and Levy Funding Overview
gemma_body:
This document provides a comprehensive financial and operational overview of the Seattle Public Library (SPL), specifically focusing on the impact of the library levy and the budget projections for 2026.

**Financial Overview**
For 2026, the library's total operating budget is projected at $102.5 million, with $35.7 million provided by the levy. The capital budget is set at $2.6 million, with $2 million coming from the levy and $605,000 from the Real Estate Excise Tax (REET). Long-term funding trends from 2020 to 2026 show a steady increase in the total budget, rising from $81.1 million to $110.1 million. While the General Fund and SPL revenue have historically been the primary funding sources, their percentage of the total budget has decreased (from 70.1% in 2020 to 60.6% in 2026), while private funding (SPLF) has increased.

**Levy Allocation and Categories**
Levy funds are distributed across several critical operational categories:
*   **Collections:** Books and materials.
*   **Hours & Access:** Maintaining public availability.
*   **Technology & Online Services:** Including cybersecurity, network infrastructure, and internet access.
*   **Helping Children:** Literacy and early learning initiatives.
*   **Building Maintenance:** Including major repairs and facility support.
*   **Administration:** Central costs and city administration.

**Collections and Accomplishments**
A significant portion of the report highlights the growth and diversification of the library's collections. Key achievements include:
*   **Digital Growth:** SPL is now one of the top 10 largest e-circulation libraries globally, with e-book checkouts nearly doubling since 2019.
*   **Expanded Access:** The library has increased language access by adding Spanish collections to all locations and introducing Dari, Pashto, Portuguese, and Ukrainian materials.
*   **Diversification:** The library maintains unique curated collections (e.g., LGBTQ+, African American, and Aviation) and has grown its digital local history collection to 33,000 items.
*   **Funding for Materials:** For the 2026 budget, "Books and Materials" are funded

title: legislation-7926747-attachment-Introduction and Referral Calendar
gemma_headline: Seattle City Council Introduction and Referral Calendar for February 24, 2026
gemma_body:
The Seattle City Council's legislative calendar for February 24, 2026, outlines eight proposed items for introduction and committee referral, focusing primarily on financial appropriations, utility infrastructure, and personnel appointments.

The proposed actions include:
*   **Financials:** An ordinance (CB 121168) to appropriate funds for claims incurred between February 9 and February 13, 2026.
*   **City Light Department:** Two ordinances (CB 121169 and CB 121170) to accept a combined total of 211 electrical distribution easements within King County and place them under the jurisdiction of the City Light Department.
*   **Appointments:** The reappointment of four members (Summer Hepburn, Todd Toshio Snider, Jeffrey G. Berry, and Michiko Starks) to the Joint Parks and City Light Apprenticeship Training Committee, with terms ending in either 2026 or 2027.
*   **Land Use:** A petition (CF 314548) from ARE-Seattle No. 10 LLC requesting the vacation of an alley located in the DT Denny’s Waterfront Addition, bounded by 4th Avenue West, West Harrison Street, Elliott Avenue West, and West Republican Street.

title: legislation-7926746-attachment-Minutes
gemma_headline: Seattle City Council Meeting Minutes: February 17, 2026
gemma_body:
On February 17, 2026, the Seattle City Council convened at City Hall under the leadership of Council President Joy Hollingsworth. Eight members were present, with Council Member Robert Kettle excused.

The meeting's primary highlights included:
*   **State of the City:** Mayor Katie B. Wilson delivered her 2026 State of the City Address, which was formally accepted and filed by the Council.
*   **Legislative Actions:** The Council unanimously adopted the Introduction & Referral Calendar and the meeting agenda. Through the Consent Calendar, the Council approved the minutes from the February 10 meeting and passed Council Bill (CB) 121162, which appropriated funds to pay claims for the week of February 2–6, 2026.
*   **Immigration Policy:** The Public Safety Committee recommended the passage of Council Bill (CB) 121158. This ordinance prohibits the City from providing non-publicly available personal information for civil immigration enforcement, reinforces that immigration enforcement is a federal matter, and updates the Seattle Municipal Code accordingly.
*   **Public Participation:** The session included a public comment period featuring testimony from 30 different individuals.

title: legislation-7926745-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
gemma_headline: Ordinance to Authorize City Payment of Bills and Payroll (February 9–13, 2026)
gemma_body:
This legislative document is a summary and fiscal note for an ordinance prepared by the Office of City Finance. The primary purpose of the legislation is to authorize the payment of various claims, including payroll and bills processed through PeopleSoft, for the period of February 9, 2026, through February 13, 2026.

Key details include:
*   **Legal Requirement:** The bill is submitted in accordance with RCW 42.24.180, which mandates City Council authorization for certain claim payments.
*   **Fiscal Impact:** There is no direct impact on the City’s overall budget, as the legislation authorizes the disbursement of funds that have already been previously appropriated by the City Council.
*   **Risk Assessment:** The document notes that failure to implement this legislation could result in the City failing to meet its legal obligations, potentially leading to increased financial and legal liability.
*   **Scope:** The ordinance affects all City departments by facilitating the payment of their respective operational expenses and employee payroll.
*   **Other Considerations:** The legislation does not involve capital improvement projects, property changes, climate change impacts, or specific racial and social justice initiatives.

title: legislation-7926745-full
gemma_headline: City of Seattle Appropriation Ordinance for Claims and Payroll (February 2026)
gemma_body:
This legislative ordinance authorizes the City of Seattle to expend funds to settle various financial obligations incurred between February 9, 2026, and February 13, 2026. The appropriation is divided into two primary categories:

1.  **General Claims and Transactions:** The ordinance approves the payment of approximately $45.08 million, consisting of $20,701,781.00 in mechanical warrants, $87,622.75 in e-payables, and $24,292,983.19 in electronic financial transactions (EFT).
2.  **Payroll Expenses:** The ordinance approves the payment of $71,890,436.38 from the City General Salary Fund to cover gross payrolls for the period ending February 10, 2026.

The text explicitly notes that all payments must remain consistent with the current amended budget and cites RCW 35.32A.090(1) to ensure that no expenditures exceed authorized budget allowances. Additionally, the ordinance ratifies any related actions taken prior to its effective date. The document was passed by the City Council on February 24, 2026.

title: legislation-7880787-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
gemma_headline: Resolution Establishing Professionalism Standards for Law Enforcement within Seattle
gemma_body:
This legislative resolution outlines the City of Seattle's expectations for the conduct of all law enforcement agencies operating within city limits, specifically targeting non-City and federal agents. The resolution asserts that any law enforcement activity occurring in Seattle should align with the city's values and the professional standards already governing the Seattle Police Department (SPD).

Key points of the resolution include:
*   **Conduct Standards:** The City emphasizes specific requirements for officers, including the use of visible identification, the prohibition of unauthorized face coverings, the obligation to render medical aid, and adherence to legal standards regarding stops and detentions.
*   **SPD Directives:** The resolution reaffirms existing SPD policies concerning the treatment of foreign nationals, immigration enforcement, and interactions with purported federal agents.
*   **Accountability:** SPD officers are directed to document and report any violations of these professionalism standards committed by other law enforcement entities.
*   **Condemnation of Federal Actions:** The text explicitly denounces recent federal immigration enforcement activities in Minneapolis, Minnesota, citing the resulting loss of life.

From a fiscal and administrative standpoint, the resolution is a statement of values and does not require new funding, capital improvement projects, or public hearings. It does not create new financial impacts for the City, as it directs the SPD to follow existing departmental policies.

title: legislation-7880787-full
gemma_headline: City of Seattle Resolution Establishing Professionalism Standards for All Law Enforcement Operating Within City Limits
gemma_body:
This resolution reaffirms the City of Seattle's commitment to public safety and civil liberties by establishing strict professional conduct expectations for all law enforcement officers operating within the city, regardless of whether they are local, state, or federal agents.

The resolution is prompted by a condemnation of "heavy-handed" and "reckless" actions by federal agents in Minneapolis, specifically citing Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) for using aggressive tactics, wearing face coverings, and failing to display identification. The City asserts that law enforcement should operate under a "guardian ethos" rather than a "warrior ethos," prioritizing the well-being of all individuals.

Key mandates and expectations outlined in the resolution include:
*   **Identification Standards:** All officers must be clearly identifiable via department emblems, name tags, and badge numbers. They are required to identify themselves upon request, with very limited exceptions for operational necessity.
*   **Face Coverings:** The use of face coverings is prohibited unless reasonably required by specific circumstances aligned with Seattle Police Department (SPD) policy.
*   **Conduct and Professionalism:** All officers, including those from outside jurisdictions, are expected to meet or exceed the professional standards and policies of the SPD.
*   **Operational Integrity:** Investigatory stops and detentions must be legally compliant and professional. Additionally, officers are directed to provide necessary medical assistance if members of the public are injured.

Ultimately, the resolution seeks to ensure that federal agencies collaborating with the city to combat guns and drugs adhere to the same transparency and accountability standards as local police to maintain public trust and safety.

title: legislation-7880790-supporting-Summary and Fiscal Note
gemma_headline: Proposed Seattle Ordinance Prohibiting Civil Immigration Enforcement Staging on City Property
gemma_body:
This proposed legislation seeks to establish a new chapter (14.125) of the Seattle Municipal Code to prohibit the staging and operation of civil immigration enforcement activities on all real property owned or controlled by the City of Seattle, to the extent allowed by law.

Key provisions of the ordinance include:
*   **Enforcement and Legal Action:** The Seattle City Attorney’s Office (CAO) is authorized to seek legal or equitable relief to stop any acts or practices that violate the prohibition.
*   **Public Notification:** The CAO and the Department of Facilities and Administrative Services (FAS) will collaborate to identify City properties that require signage notifying the public of the prohibition. Priority for signage will be given to facilities providing essential public services, such as health care and child care.
*   **Financial Impact:** Signage production will be handled by the Seattle Department of Transportation (SDOT) using existing appropriations. However, the total cost remains indeterminate until FAS completes its property review; if funds are insufficient, SDOT may need to defer other projects. Additionally, the CAO may need to redirect staff resources if significant legal proceedings are required to enforce the ordinance.
*   **Justification:** The legislation is intended to ensure that undocumented immigrants feel safe accessing public spaces and essential services without fear of immigration enforcement staging.

title: legislation-7880790-supporting-Central Staff Memo
gemma_headline: Proposed Legislation to Prohibit Civil Immigration Enforcement Staging on City-Owned Property
gemma_body:
This memorandum, authored by Analyst Greg Doss for the Public Safety Committee, outlines Council Bill (CB) 121164. The proposed legislation seeks to prohibit the use of City-owned and controlled properties for civil immigration enforcement operations. Specifically, it would ban the use of such properties for assembling, mobilizing, or deploying personnel, vehicles, and equipment, as well as for logistical coordination or surveillance related to civil immigration enforcement.

The memo provides important legal context, noting that while immigration enforcement is a federal function, several other major U.S. cities (including Los Angeles, Chicago, and Spokane) have implemented similar restrictions on the use of municipal property by federal agents. It further notes that this bill aligns with a recent Directive issued by Mayor Katie Wilson on January 29, 2026, and a similar Executive Order from King County Executive Girmay Zahilay.

Crucially, the legislation does not apply to the public right-of-way, nor does it restrict federal officials who are acting under a judicial warrant, a court order, or those engaged in criminal (rather than civil) enforcement activities. Additionally, a proposed technical amendment (Amendment 1) will be offered to ensure the prohibition is specifically restricted to "staging activities."

title: legislation-7880790-supporting-Amendment 1
gemma_headline: Amendment to CB 121164 Restricting City Property Use for Immigration Enforcement Staging
gemma_body:
Sponsored by Councilmember Kettle, this technical amendment to CB 121164 clarifies that the City of Seattle's prohibition on civil immigration enforcement applies specifically to "staging activities." 

Under the proposed Chapter 14.125 of the Seattle Municipal Code, City-owned and controlled real property may not be used to assemble, mobilize, or deploy personnel, vehicles, or equipment for civil immigration operations, nor for the logistical coordination or surveillance associated with such operations. Notably, this prohibition does not extend to public rights-of-way used for pedestrian and vehicular traffic.

The amendment explicitly states that these restrictions do not interfere with the enforcement of criminal law, the execution of lawful judicial warrants, or any rights granted under state or federal law. Additionally, the City Attorney’s Office is authorized to seek injunctive or other legal relief to prevent violations of this ordinance.

title: legislation-7880790-full
gemma_headline: City of Seattle Proposes Ban on Civil Immigration Enforcement Staging on City-Owned Property
gemma_body:
This proposed ordinance seeks to establish a new chapter (Chapter 14.125) of the Seattle Municipal Code to prohibit the use of City-owned and controlled real property for the purposes of civil immigration enforcement staging. 

Under the proposed law, federal authorities would be barred from using City property—including parks, plazas, garages, vacant lots, and the Seattle Center—to assemble, mobilize, or deploy personnel, vehicles, and equipment, or to conduct surveillance and logistical coordination for immigration operations. The ordinance specifically excludes public rights-of-way (such as streets used for pedestrian and vehicular traffic) from this prohibition.

The legislation justifies this restriction by citing the City's status as a "Welcoming City," its commitment to protecting immigrant and refugee communities, and the Tenth Amendment's protection against federal commandeering of local resources. 

The ordinance includes two key limitations:
1. It does not interfere with the execution of lawful judicial warrants or the enforcement of criminal law.
2. It does not restrict the lawful use of City property for any purpose unrelated to immigration enforcement staging.

Finally, the ordinance empowers the Seattle City Attorney’s Office to seek injunctive relief or other legal remedies in court to prevent violations of these restrictions.

title: legislation-7924369-supporting-Seattle Fire Department Briefing
gemma_headline: Overview of the SFD Mobile Integrated Health (MIH) Program
gemma_body:
The Mobile Integrated Health (MIH) program is a specialized initiative designed to reduce the burden on standard SFD Operations by managing low-acuity alarms and providing targeted support for "high utilizers" of emergency services, as well as individuals experiencing chronic medical, behavioral, or social crises.

**Program Evolution and Growth**
The program has evolved from a 2011 Vulnerable Adult Program into a comprehensive service. Key milestones include the launch of "Health One" in 2019, the phased rollout of additional units (H2, H99, H98), and the 2024 launch of an ARNP program in partnership with Harborview Medical Center (HMC). The program plans to expand weekend services by 2026 and increase staffing levels (Case Managers and FF/EMTs) through 2027.

**Operational Structure**
MIH operates city-wide from Monday to Sunday (09:00–19:00), utilizing a diverse fleet of vehicles including crew cab pickups, wheelchair vans, and ambulances. Staffing consists of a multidisciplinary team of Case Managers, Firefighter/EMTs, and ARNPs. The program responds to three primary triggers:
*   Direct 911 dispatches for behavioral health crises and suicidality.
*   Requests from Live Operations.
*   Self-dispatch for overdoses, new referrals, or existing client follow-ups.

**Service Capabilities and Case Management**
Unlike traditional emergency responses, MIH focuses on longitudinal, client-centered care. With an average scene time of 42 minutes, teams provide EMS care, crisis intervention, and essential supplies (food/clothing), often utilizing "warm handoffs" to alternative transport destinations. A critical component of the program is the integration of HSD case managers, who help clients access state-level benefits and long-term care to move beyond episodic emergency interactions.

**Specialized Responses**
The program maintains a flexible posture for severe weather, where it can transition to 24/7 operations. During these periods, the focus shifts to life safety, outreach, and mass sheltering, utilizing a Mobile Ambulance Bus in coordination with the EOC and KCRHA.

title: legislation-7924369-supporting-CARE (Community Assisted Response and Engagement) Briefing
gemma_headline: Seattle CARE Department 2026 Public Safety Committee Briefing: Program Expansion and Operational Overview
gemma_body:
Chief Amy Barden provided a comprehensive briefing on the evolution and current state of the Seattle Community Crisis Responder (CARE) program. The presentation outlines the department's transition from a small pilot program to a citywide crisis response system.

**Program Evolution and Staffing**
The CARE Department has grown significantly since its launch in October 2023. Starting with 6 responders providing limited coverage in the Downtown Activation Team (DAT) area, the program expanded to 24 responders providing citywide coverage for 10 hours per day as of April 2025. Looking forward, the department aims to implement data-driven staffing to achieve 24/7 coverage, with a goal of increasing citywide staffing through four new cohorts by the end of 2026.

**Dispatch Protocols**
The department has shifted from a system reliant on officer discretion (secondary response) to one based on "Direct Dispatch" using standing criteria. 
*   **Solo Dispatch:** Utilized for low-risk scenarios such as welfare checks, "down persons," and individuals in crisis.
*   **Dual Dispatch:** Used for similar scenarios but triggered when there are higher risks, such as the presence of minors or specific environmental factors.
*   **Exclusions:** Solo dispatch is prohibited in cases involving private residences, vehicles, aggressive behavior, visible weapons/narcotics, or indications that a crime has occurred.

**Operational Impact and Services**
Since inception, the CARE team has handled a wide variety of calls—over 50 unique call types—with the most common being welfare checks, assisting the public, and responding to "down persons." The responders provide three primary tiers of service:
1.  **Interpersonal Support:** Including de-escalation, emotional support, and safety planning.
2.  **Referrals and Resources:** Connecting individuals to local services and providing transportation.
3.  **Basic Needs:** Distributing essential supplies such as food, water, and hygiene items.

The briefing concludes by emphasizing a future vision of a "full spectrum" first-response model that incorporates meaningful short-term follow-up and case management services.

title: legislation-7924369-supporting-Seattle Police Department Briefing
gemma_headline: Seattle Police Department Presentation on Diversified Response Strategies
gemma_body:
On February 24, 2026, the Seattle Police Department (SPD) presented its "Diversified Response" framework to the Public Safety Committee. This initiative aims to enhance public safety by utilizing specialized teams to handle non-criminal and behavioral health emergencies, thereby diverting specific types of calls away from traditional sworn police response.

The presentation highlighted two primary diversified response units:

**1. Community Service Officers (CSOs)**
CSOs are non-commissioned, unarmed officers who serve as liaisons between the SPD and the community. They do not enforce criminal laws but instead respond to non-criminal, non-medical 911 calls. Their primary duties include:
*   **Resource Connection:** Linking community members to essential services such as housing, food, elder care, and mental health support.
*   **Operational Support:** Assisting patrol officers with transports, victim support, mediation (e.g., landlord/tenant disputes), and distributing emergency supplies.
*   **Community Collaboration:** Partnering with city agencies, businesses, and grassroots organizations to foster positive relationships.
*   **Growth:** The unit currently consists of 20 CSOs and 4 supervisors. In 2025, the program saw significant growth, with a 100% increase in CAD event responses and a 35% increase in requests from patrol officers compared to 2024.

**2. Crisis Response Team (CRT)**
The CRT utilizes a "co-responder model," pairing specially trained police officers with Mental Health Professionals (MHPs). The team's objectives include:
*   **Holistic Intervention:** Managing encounters with individuals experiencing behavioral health crises to divert them from the criminal justice system toward appropriate healthcare resources.
*   **Violence Prevention:** Utilizing Extreme Risk Protection Orders (ERPO) and Denied Firearm Transactions (DFT) to remove weapons from individuals deemed a risk to themselves or others.
*   **Staffing:** The team currently operates Monday through Friday (6:00 am to 6:00 pm) with 4 MHPs and 5 officers, with plans to add more personnel in 2026.

Overall, these programs reflect the SPD's broader priorities of crime prevention and community engagement by ensuring that the most appropriate professional—whether a social service liaison or a mental health expert—responds to the specific needs of the citizen.

On Monday, January 12, 2026, the Seattle City Council held a briefing at City Hall presided over by Council President Joy Hollingsworth. The meeting commenced at 2:02 p.m. with eight council members present and one member, Debora Juarez, excused.

title: legislation-7829604-attachment-January 26, 2026

gemma_body:
The Seattle City Council held a briefing on Monday, January 26, 2026, presided over by Council President Pro Tem Strauss. The meeting commenced at 2:01 p.m. with four members present, two excused, and three arriving late. 

During the public portion of the meeting, the Council approved the minutes from the January 12, 2026, briefing. The committee also reviewed two information items: an update regarding the State Legislative Session (Inf 2821) and a preview of upcoming City Council and regional committee actions (Inf 2819). 

At 3:07 p.m., the Council entered a closed Executive Session to discuss matters regarding pending, potential, or actual litigation. The session concluded at 4:37 p.m., at which time the meeting was adjourned.
